title: 1777: The Saratoga Campaign
author: Historical Fact
content: The British Army was forced to surrender after its supply lines from Canada were severed and it was caught in a geographic trap in the Hudson River valley. It was the first time an entire British field army was captured.
action: Record: The dependence of a modern, professional force on continuous, vulnerable supply lines.

title: 1864: Sherman’s March to the Sea
author: Historical Fact
content: General Sherman abandoned his own supply lines entirely, forcing his army to 'live off the land.' He successfully moved 62,000 men across 285 miles of hostile territory with no central logistics train.
action: Record: The viability of extreme operational independence when logistics are decoupled from a central base.

title: 1918: The Meuse-Argonne Offensive
author: Historical Fact
content: The largest and deadliest operation in U.S. military history. 1.2 million American soldiers were deployed, requiring the movement of 28,000 tons of ammunition daily. It brought the U.S. industrial machine directly to the front line.
action: Record: The transition point where industrial throughput capacity became the primary determinant of battlefield success.

title: 1942: The Battle of Midway (Station HYPO)
author: Historical Fact
content: U.S. Navy codebreakers identified the target (Midway) by analyzing the frequency of traffic patterns and deciphering the Japanese 'AF' code. This allowed the U.S. to position its limited assets at the exact point of enemy convergence.
action: Record: The force-multiplier effect of superior information regarding the timing and location of an opponent's intent.

title: 1950: The Inchon Landing (Operation Chromite)
author: Historical Fact
content: General MacArthur conducted an amphibious landing at Inchon despite extreme tidal ranges (up to 30 feet) and narrow approach channels. The success relied on an incredibly tight 3-hour window of high tide for the landing craft.
action: Record: The necessity of precise, high-stakes operational timing when working against extreme environmental constraints.
